stem: A data set has a mean of 20 and a standard deviation of 4. A researcher creates a new data set by first multiplying every value in the original data set by 3 and then adding 5 to each result. What is the standard deviation of the new data set?
explanation:
STEP A — No individual values are given; the question operates on the summary statistics of the data set.

STEP B — Understand how linear transformations affect the mean and standard deviation.
  General rule: if every value x in a data set is transformed to y = a·x + b, then
    New mean = a · (original mean) + b
    New standard deviation = |a| · (original standard deviation)
  The additive constant b shifts every value by the same amount, so all deviations from the mean remain unchanged — the standard deviation is unaffected by adding a constant.
  The multiplicative factor a stretches every deviation from the mean by the factor |a|, so the standard deviation is multiplied by |a|.

STEP C — Apply the rule to this problem.
  Transformation: y = 3x + 5, so a = 3 and b = 5.
  New standard deviation = |3| × 4 = 12
  New mean = 3 × 20 + 5 = 65 (not asked, but confirms the transformation is correctly understood)

STEP F — Standard deviation is used conceptually: scaling multiplies SD by the scale factor; shifting leaves SD unchanged.

STEP H — Correct answer = 12, which is choice C. ✓

STEP I — Distractor analysis:
  Choice A (4): MISCONCEPTION — SD-unchanged error. The student believes that multiplying every value by 3 does not affect the spread of the data, treating the multiplication the same way as adding a constant. A student who knows that adding a constant leaves SD unchanged may overgeneralize this rule to multiplication as well.
  Choice B (7): MISCONCEPTION — wrong operation on SD. The student adds the multiplier to the SD instead of multiplying: 4 + 3 = 7. This student confuses an additive shift with a multiplicative stretch, applying the wrong arithmetic operation to the SD.
  Choice D (17): MISCONCEPTION — applies the full transformation to SD. The student correctly multiplies the SD by 3 to get 12, but then also adds the constant 5, computing 3 × 4 + 5 = 17. This student does not recognize that adding a constant to every data value does not affect deviations from the mean and therefore does not affect the standard deviation.

distractorRationale.A: SD-unchanged error: the student incorrectly treats the multiplicative factor like an additive constant, concluding the standard deviation is unaffected by multiplying every value by 3.
distractorRationale.B: Wrong operation on SD: the student adds the multiplier (3) to the original SD instead of multiplying, computing 4 + 3 = 7, confusing a multiplicative stretch with an additive shift.
distractorRationale.C: Correct answer: the new SD = |3| × 4 = 12, because multiplying every value by 3 multiplies all deviations from the mean by 3, while adding 5 to every value shifts the mean but does not change any deviation.
distractorRationale.D: Full-transformation-applied-to-SD error: the student correctly multiplies the SD by 3 to get 12, but then incorrectly also adds the constant 5, computing 3×4+5 = 17, failing to recognize that the additive constant does not affect spread.
